speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape four with Mrs. Rachela Warszauer Schlufman, August 29, 1995. Mrs. Schlufman, you were telling me about the rules, the laws in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember how my father felt when he had to go to the barber and cut his beard off. Because he would be very risky to walk on the street with a beard. He didn't want to do it himself, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was so much a Hasidic. How could he cut his beard himself? So he let somebody else do it. I don't know where he went, really, but I assume he went to a barber. And he came home. You know what he did? He took a scarf, he wrapped it around his face. I'll never forget that. My father walked into the house, shaved, you know? And cut off the beard, cut off the peyos, I couldn't recognize my father. His face was so pale. And he-- my mother didn't look at him. And then little by little, we got accustomed to my father's face without the beard. So I took all the pain, because I was the only girl left home, you know, with Moishe and with Shmulek, while Berl was away already. He went because of his-- his-- his position. He was fixing watches. He thought, will be better if he didn't have a house where to put them.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to Ostrowiec, to another city. And he was dealing with the-- he was fixing for the German people, for the army, you know, watches. And he was doing very well. And we were very happy to hear that. And-- and Leibl was on the farm. So Zisman and Moishe and Shmulek, the three of them, were staying in ghetto apartment with us. At the time, living in ghetto, it was very, very difficult. Moishe had a job with the Germans bringing-- working by coals-- you know, coal, coal, what you-- what you heat the oven, coal. Not in coal mines, but loading coals on the wagons from the trains going from one city to the other. So he did that job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every time when he came home, he will steal-- you know, try to-- to-- to steal a piece of coal and hide under his jacket. With that little coal, we could burn our home, you know, a fire. Without that, you cannot cook. Before the war, the Pollacks used to bring wood to the city. But during the ghetto, there was no wood. Where did you take wood? People used to bend down chairs-- break down chairs to have a piece of wood to burn a fire. The [? Poles ?] didn't bring you in, so where could you take it? Where? Didn't come from-- from-- from the sky. So we were blessed with that, he got that job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shmulek, that little boy, you know, he was so young. At the time, he was 12 already. And then he got a job sorting out potatoes by the Germans, the bad ones from the good ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he used to sneak home potatoes. That was a blessing. You know, you can make many dishes from potatoes, many dishes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can make latkes. You can cook potatoes. You can eat mashed potatoes. You can make a potato soup, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at least we didn't go so hungry yet, because of these two brothers. I was blessed. And we even helped our neighbor with these potatoes, you know? So that was really the greatest thing in ghetto, what we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in there, they used to give us a lot of bread, the Germans. We used to stay in line. That was very, very difficult to stay in line.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I stood-- you stood and came to the end. They say, it's no more bread. I came home, said to my mother, it's no more bread. What can you say? It's no more bread. No more bread. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very, very hard. Ghetto life was so hard, sometimes harder than in concentration. But at least we were with our parents. There were no food. Food, we could go for days without a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to lay on the-- on the bed and feeling so weak and dizzy that I was thinking-- I wasn't thinking of that. I was thinking that my heart will stop, you know? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a terrible life. It was a terrible-- but until my brother got that job on the potatoes. That helped us through. He used to bring home some a piece of bread what the Germans used to give him, you know? He used to bring home. And it was like a feast in the house. He brought home potatoes. He brought home a piece of bread. And Zisman was also trying from his job to-- to-- to do something. But the Germans, to bring home, to sneak home, it was taking, you know-- stealing. Without stealing, you couldn't survive, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- you had holidays during this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The holidays--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Passover like in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover. My only Passover-- what I remember was-- was two Passovers, actually. One Passover was when my mother got very sick. That was in 1941-- 19-- 1940, when she was very sick. She was ailing, but not really sick. But I had to do all the work at my age to-- to try whatever my brother's got into, some of the food. It's like I say, we got some matzos. They baked matzos in ghetto. The big help was my Aunt Hindi with Rachmiel, with her husband Rachmiel. They were a big help for us. That's why I mention them in the book-- because in my book while I was writing. I-- she was my favorite tante. Whenever I came to her house, first step, she would give me a piece of bread with a piece of cheese, I remember, and a glass of milk. She was very, very good to us. And I bless her-- I bless her now. God, she should be in heaven, you know, for that. But she did the good deeds, and-- and so did Rachmiel, too. My aunt--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about Passover.

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover was very quiet in the house. We had to say-- we had to conduct very quietly. We lit the candles. We had matzos. We had some potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father got raisins. He made his own little wine, you know? And my mother got the beets. She made the borscht. And what else? And that-- and eggs. The farmers, you know, used to sneak into the ghetto and bring eggs, which it was a blessing. So we, you know, we had that, not too much, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't need eggs. But we just-- we needed for [INAUDIBLE], for things. And one, we could, you know, follow the custom of Passover. And that was-- looking around at the table, my mother used to cry. She used to see-- she used to see that was the first [NON-ENGLISH] after the-- at beginning of the war. She had Zisman. And Berl was away. She had Moishe and Shmulek and me. And from eight, she had four. [SOBBING] She used to cry. But the most difficult things was in ghetto for me to witness of losing my two brothers, Zisman one, and Berl, the other. That was the most painful thing for me. My mother was struck with a heart condition. She was very sick. I called the Jewish doctor in ghetto, Dr. Feldman, and I asked him to come to help my mother. He says-- he examines her. He says-- calls me out, and he says, Miss. Warszauer, your mother's not going to live. Most what she can live is two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, to think that I'm going to be an orphan, that I'm going to lose my mother, I could not think about it. I says, how can you say that? How can you say that my mother's going to die? Don't you believe in God? He says, if you believe in God, he says to me, have hope. And he walked away from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back into the house. I looked at my mother. I could not think and imagine that I'm going to lose my mother in two days. I-- I was crying in my heart. I cried in outside. I went to my Aunt Hindi and to Rachmiel. They had some protection in the committee to get a permit to go on the Aryan side. And I told them what the Dr. Feldman says. Ah, we have another doctor. But he's out of-- he's a Polish doctor, Dr. [? Bujinski. ?] If I go to him, but we have-- we need a permit. If you go-- if Dr. [? Bujinski, ?] I don't know if he will come to the ghetto. He wouldn't risk his life. I says, but maybe you can get a permit for me. They got me a permit. And as a young girl, I walked and walked and walked about a few miles scary, you know, seeing the Germans, the soldiers, until I got to that Polish [? Dr. Bujinski. ?] I came in, his wife Dr. [? Roma, ?] she stood in the front. And she asked me what I want. I told her that my mother's very sick. And they knew Dr. Feldman and-- and what Dr. Feldman says. And perhaps Dr. [? Bujinksi's ?] a known doctor in Skarzysko and maybe he could help my mother. She looked at me. And she says, wait here. Let me ask Dr. [? Bujinski. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He came out with his white uniform with his cigar in his hand. And he looked at me from toe to top, to head. And he says to me, well, he says, how old is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him, she is not 50 yet. But what is her-- Dr. Feldman says her heart is so bad that she's going to die any time. Perhaps you can-- she's blowing up with water like a balloon, laying in bed. He says, well, I am not God. And I don't think I can help your mother. But if you believe in God, have faith. He took out a few pills. He says, go home. Tell her to take that twice a day. And if she doesn't get better, you come here and I will go with you and look after your mother and see how she's doing. I blessed him. I says, God bless you. I shook his hand-- hands. And I kissed his hand. And I says, oh, God, he's an angel. Not like Dr. Feldman, a Jewish doctor, and he told me that my mother's going to die. At least--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the pills work?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pills-- My mother started to take pills. And the water started to come out. Gallons of water came out. And I changed out every time the sheets. And I rinsed it the water, you know. And I dried it outside and put the sheet back for my mother and days-- days after days. But while she was so sick--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she get better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen to that. While she was so sick, she pulled her sheet, her cover. And I sat near her. Because I sat. And I took a chair, and I slept the whole night on that chair to watch over my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, mama, why are you pulling that cover? She says, you know why? I had a dream. She spoke so slowly. I had a dream that Berl, my son Berl, is crying. He calls for me, Berl. Mama, I says, Berl is-- is in-- in a different city, you know? I forgot the city, you know. Ostrowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrowiec. And I says, he's doing fine. He's doing fine. She says, no, I heard his voice. He's crying, mama. She had a premonition that something is wrong with her son Berl. Well, one time when my mother got better, day after day, you know, with the pills, and I-- I walked-- I went to [? Dr. Bujinski. ?] I thanked him so much for helping my mother. Because these pills made release the water. My mother still had heart-- pains in her heart and her chest. But she put on cold compresses. She survived. She walked around. She started to walk. And she started, you know, to do some-- a little bit cooking. And then I was so happy to see my mother standing on her feet and doing on her own a little bit. I was so happy to have my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, all of a sudden-- in most of the daytime, I-- we had a bench outside. At that bench, I used to sit and dream what will happen. When will the war will end? When will the sun shine for us? When will I have enough food? When will I be able to go to a store like I used to go when my mother used to send me to bring her bread, rolls, milk, eggs. Will that ever be again? Is that a dream? I dreamed. All of a sudden, a man came over. And he says to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. I know him. His name was Izrak. I says, Izrak, what are you doing here? You're supposed to--

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you know, I'm glad you're sitting outside. He knocked at the door, but he didn't find me. So he find me outside. But I'm glad I can talk to you outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened, Izrak? My heart was pounding. I was afraid. I was afraid to face people. I was afraid to hear bad news.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, look, your mother-- don't ever tell your mother. I know your mother's very sick. I was working at the railway station. We had a railway station. The trains were going back and forth. A train stopped with a lot of Jewish people. One man, an older Jew, he says, walked over to-- to the-- not the-- no. I am mixing up. A young fellow that was your brother, I recognized him, bent down from the window and called me over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, I am [? Barrick ?] Warszauer. Do you know who I am? Izrak says, yes, you are Warszauer's son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Please, tell my sister Rachel, Rachela, that I am on this wagon. I don't know where the Germans are taking me on my last destination, where they're going to do with me, what they're going to do with me. I was caught. I heard my mother was sick. I took off the armband. And I took a chance to ride on the train. And two Pollacks identified me that I am a Jew. And they gave me over to the Germans. And they arrested me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why this older man, who was my grandfather, who was slaughtering two chickens for Passover in [NON-ENGLISH] was caught from our Polish neighbor, and he was arrested was on the same train and listening to Berl, to his grandson, saying-- and he says to him, listen, you are Warszauer's son? Your name is Berl? And Berl didn't see his grandfather since he was a kid. He didn't recognize him. Now, he was 19. Grandfather didn't recognize his grandson either. You know? He's a man, 19. So he says, you're Warszauer's son? And the man-- and this older man says to him, oh, God in heaven. What are you doing among these people here? You are my grandson. You are my daughter's son, Beila's son. Well, Berl says, God, grandfather. You're Grandfather Jacob, [? Yakov, ?] my mother's father. What are you doing here? And-- and this-- this neighbor of ours is standing and listening how these two, the young man with his grandfather, the grandfather, you know, with his grandson, embracing each other on that train. So they say--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were sent away to a destination where we don't know-- perhaps to Auschwitz. That was the only place. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Izrak came back to tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Izrak was telling me that story. I looked at Izrak's eyes. And I says, Izrak, how can I tell this? He says, God forbid, you should never bring these words to your mother and father. Forget about it. If your mother is going to hear about her father and about her son Berl, she's going to die on the spot. I wiped my tears. I sat for a few minutes. My-- my eyes should be clear from the tears.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked into the house. I looked at my mother. I looked at her. And I says, ma, when you dreamed about Berl, what kind of dream? Did he yell? Did he [INAUDIBLE]? How did he look to you? He looked to me like in a shadow, she says. And he cried. He yelled. I just pray to God that nothing is wrong with him. That dream, she says, it's weird. Why did I have that dream if he's doing well?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had such a bad premonition about her son. She was like a mind reader. She was such a wise woman, my mother. Whatever she says, whatever she mentioned, I swallowed her wise words until now. Now, my brother was gone-- now, to get the life, get the life. My little-- my little cousin, one time we're sitting in the house, a little boy knocks at the door, 10-year-old little boy. Thought, he's a beggar, torn, ripped up his legs, bleeding, his face scratched up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I says, I haven't got bread to give you, I says. He says, I am not a beggar. I am your cousin. My father is your-- is your father's brother. You know? I says, what? What are you talking about? He says, yes. My father-- my father's brother, his name was Chaskel. He was taken to the Polish Army. And he was killed before the war ended when the Germans came in. They find him dead under a tree in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife, my aunt, with three little children, and Zisman was the oldest, 10-years-old, was struck with typhus in ghetto-- in Warsaw ghetto, dying from hunger. Her children with a temperature, dying from hunger. She [NON-ENGLISH]. And with her last breath, she says, Zisman, my-- my kind, perhaps you have a chance. We have family in Skarzysko. She didn't mean us. She didn't know that we will come from Wachock to Skarzysko. She meant my-- my father's brother, my Aunt Hindi. That was her husband's sister. You go to Aunt Hindi and to Rachmiel. Go to Skarzysko. And you-- you-- perhaps, you can live. Perhaps, you can help yourself. She didn't want to tell them that we have no chance. We are dying. The boy did see the last breath of his little sister, his little brother. They're just breathing and dying. He picked himself up, walking from Warsaw to Skarzysko. It took him six weeks, you know, by walking. He slept on the farms. And he ate the potatoes what they gave for the cows. He didn't identify himself that he's a Jewish boy. And he hustled to get into [INAUDIBLE], to Hindi. And from Hindi, he came-- Hindi told him that we are here. And he came into our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I called my father. And I says, is this Uncle Chaskel's son is here? Zisman, he got the same name as my brother Zisman. All the cousins carried the same name as the same grandfather. And my father walked over with my mother. They couldn't recognize that poor kid. My father's first step was, how did the father die? When he told him that somebody told him that he identified his father, he was dead. And they buried him with many, many soldiers together.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father took out right away the [INAUDIBLE]. And he started to pray in his heart. Perhaps, he said [NON-ENGLISH], whatever he said. I looked at my father's pale face. I could not understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened, [SOB] the same night he slept in our house. I gave him my bed in the kitchen. I slept with my mother. He was-- in middle of the night, he was [INAUDIBLE]. My mother went down. And she says, why-- what's the matter with you? His forehead was hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother knew the symptoms already. She put her hand to his forehead. She says, oh, my god, he must have typhus. Probably he brought the typhus from Warsaw. And this is so-- you know, it's like fire, one catches from the other. She says to me-- she woke me up. And she says, Rachela, don't walk over to him. Because the kid has typhus. Zisman has typhus.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, what is typhus? Did I know about typhus? My mother knew from the First World War. I didn't know about that sickness, about that disease.

speaker: interviewee
text: She warned my brother Moishe and my brother, you know, Shmulek. [NON-ENGLISH], please stay away from Zisman. Because he has typhus, and this is like fire. That's all what I need that you children, you should catch typhus. Then we all finished. But my heart bleeded to look at my cousin Zisman. His-- his cheeks were so red, so skinny, so pale. And he [INAUDIBLE]. So I took the towel and wet it in water. And I put it to his forehead. My mother didn't see it, but I did it back and forth. Sure enough, next day I could not get up from bed. My face was blushed, flushed. I felt hot. I felt dizzy. I couldn't-- I felt my head is falling off. My mother looks at me. She says, oh, God in heaven. God in heaven, my [INAUDIBLE], you catch typhus. My child caught the typhus from Zisman.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I am going to do? God in heaven, what I'm going to do? They're going to kill us. They will come in and burn the whole house with us together. Because the Germans were afraid for typhus. They shouldn't-- Catch it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they shouldn't catch it. But then this was like a fire. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they quarantine you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Shmulek got up the same symptoms. Ma, he called her, give me a little water. My mother-- we didn't have a thermometer to-- to-- to see how much temperature. But feeling the heat, that my-- I couldn't-- the pain in my head, I thought I'd get [INAUDIBLE]. And Shmulek was screaming, too. And Zisman was screaming, too. Moishe was the only one. My mother pushed him out. She touched his forehead. He didn't have temperature. She said, Moishe, Zisman, go to Aunt Hindi. [NON-ENGLISH] don't stay in the house. Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we three of us got very sick, but you cannot hide rumors-- every little thing, neighbors. Right away the ghetto people knew that my cousin Zisman came to the ghetto and brought typhus to us. Right away, I remember clearly that pain, that shame, as being 16-years-old, a girl in that age. You know? I was so alone to myself. I felt so devastated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two Jewish policeman walked in. And they told us we have to be placed where-- in a little-- in a-- it was like in a-- in a place what used to be before the war, a [NON-ENGLISH]. And they made it like a little hospital there. You have to be placed there. And your home, they put a bar on the door, 'forbidden to enter our house.' By the--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now.
